lesson.menu_title: Crisis Communication -- When Things Go Wrong
lesson.subtitle: Aula 14 -- Crisis Communication -- When Things Go Wrong
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rafael Pelizaro | Aula 14 -- Crisis Communication

listenings.text: Hi team, this is Rafael with a quick update on last night's incident. At about eleven p.m., while most of us were sleeping, the payment service went down. Customers were trying to check out, but the system was returning errors, so we opened a war room and declared a sev-one. When the on-call engineer joined, the database was already running out of memory. We worked together to remediate the problem: we restarted the service, and the outage ended at around midnight. The disruption lasted about an hour, and it affected roughly two thousand customers. I have updated the status page and posted an apology. Tomorrow we will run a postmortem to understand the root cause and prevent it next time. Thank you for staying calm while we were fixing it, and please send me any details you noticed during the incident.

Hi Rafael, this is Marina with a short follow-up before the postmortem tomorrow. I reviewed the logs from last night. While the team was restarting the service, I noticed that the memory alert had been firing for twenty minutes before we declared the incident. So the real lesson is about detection, not the fix. The severity was correct, the war room worked well, and you communicated clearly on the status page. My only suggestion is this: if the alert was so loud, we should have escalated sooner. For the postmortem, I will prepare a timeline of the outage, the customer impact, and three action items to reduce the disruption next time. The most important one is a better alert, so we catch the problem while it is small. See you tomorrow, and thank you for leading the response so calmly.

extra_audio.text: First, confirm the incident is real and check how many customers it is affecting. Next, open a war room and set the severity, so everyone knows how urgent it is. Then, work with the on-call engineer to remediate the problem and end the outage. After that, update the status page and post a clear, honest message for customers. Finally, run a postmortem to find the root cause, and write action items so the same disruption does not happen again.
